tested_predictions.hypothesis: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥3pp macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471, F=1.04) carries strong nonlinear signal that LR cannot capture while tree-based models leverage it
tested_predictions.basis: Top MI feature Total_EMI_per_month has MI=0.5471 but low F=1.04 indicating nonlinear separability; no high correlations mean tree splits exploit this unique signal

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥1.1× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary because its MI=0.5471 vs next-best MI=0.5187
tested_predictions.basis: MI concentration at top two features (0.5471 vs 0.5187) and Total_EMI_per_month’s unique nonlinear signal

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Delay_from_due_date will have the highest absolute standardized coefficient in the final logistic regression model with |coef| ≥2× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary because its ANOVA F=11471.89 vs F=1903.07
tested_predictions.basis: Delay_from_due_date shows by far the strongest linear class separation (F=11471.89)

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) with Standard→Good as the dominant confusion because Standard’s means on Monthly_Inhand_Salary (μ=4242) and Delay_from_due_date (μ=19.75) sit between Good and Poor
supported_conjectures.basis: Class-means overlap: Standard is sandwiched between Good and Poor on the top-2 MI features; F-statistics show strong but imperfect separation
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Per-class recall and confusion matrix at evaluate-models showing Standard→Good vs Standard→Poor rates

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Rows with missing Type_of_Loan will be predicted as Poor at ≥60% rate because missingness correlates with target (max_target_dist_diff=19.51pp)
supported_conjectures.basis: Type_of_Loan has 11.41% missing and max target distribution difference of 19.51pp indicating MNAR signal
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Segment-level predicted class distribution and precision for missing vs non-missing Type_of_Loan at evaluate-models

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The ratio Monthly_Inhand_Salary/Total_EMI_per_month will be more discriminative than either feature alone because the two are uncorrelated (no |r|>0.8) and capture debt burden residual
exploratory_leads.basis: Top MI features are independent and combine linear (salary) and nonlinear (EMI) signals
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Engineer the ratio feature, retrain models, and compare its SHAP rank vs individual components

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The interaction Delay_from_due_date × Num_Credit_Inquiries will reveal credit‐stress clusters because neither is highly correlated yet both signal delinquency pressure
exploratory_leads.basis: Delay_from_due_date (F=11471.89, MI=0.1215) and Num_Credit_Inquiries (F=6.26, MI=0.1114) carry complementary information
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Add the product term to feature set and evaluate gain in tree split importance or logistic regression coefficient significance

eda_hypotheses.model_selection_prediction: XGBoost will achieve macro_f1 ≥0.75, outperforming Logistic Regression by ≥3pp because high MI in nonlinear Total_EMI_per_month and independent top features favor boosted trees
eda_hypotheses.class_struggle_prediction: Standard will have the lowest recall with Standard→Good as the dominant confusion pair due to overlapping class-conditional means on the top features

Good.diagnosis: Recall=0.83, highest among classes. Actual Good samples are misclassified as Standard (523) 5× more than as Poor (104), indicating the Good/Standard boundary is the main confusion. High recall but low precision (0.55) shows many Poor and Standard samples fall into the Good region, suggesting feature overlap where tree splits overpredict Good.

Poor.diagnosis: Recall=0.77, moderate difficulty. Poor samples wrongly go to Standard (766) vs Good (562), a 1.36× tilt toward Standard. This indicates the Poor/Standard boundary is thinner than Poor/Good in feature space, likely due to similar EMI_to_Salary patterns for Poor and Standard.

Standard.diagnosis: Recall=0.62, lowest. Of the 4,572 Standard errors, 2,133 go to Poor vs 1,933 to Good—a slight asymmetry toward Poor—implying Standard shares more overlap with Poor on the key nonlinear splits. Overall high error spread across both neighbors reflects boundary ambiguity.

capacity_analysis.logistic_regression: Underfitting: best C=0.042 (heavy L2) and CV-test macro_f1 gap ~1.3pp indicate capacity-limited linear decision surface. Its macro_f1 (0.651) lags XGBoost by 4.4pp, showing it misses nonlinear interactions.
capacity_analysis.random_forest: Well-fitted: max_depth=11, CV-test gap ~0.4pp. Matches test weighted_f1 (0.685) closely with no sign of overfitting, indicating tree depth sufficient to capture most nonlinear structure.
capacity_analysis.xgboost: Well-fitted with remaining capacity: learning_rate=0.017, colsample≈0.96, subsample≈0.72. CV-test gap ~2.4pp but training/validation mlogloss decrease in lockstep without divergence, showing no overfitting and potential gains from more boosting rounds.

confidence_analysis.summary: LR has narrow self-knowledge (correct 0.669 vs wrong 0.641, gap 0.028). RF shows moderate calibration (0.732 vs 0.655, gap 0.077). XGBoost exhibits strong competence signals (0.782 vs 0.669, gap 0.113), especially per-class: Standard correct confidence 0.796 vs wrong 0.637 (gap 0.159), suggesting usable thresholds for high-stakes decisions.

training_diagnostics.learning_curve_interpretation: XGBoost training and validation mlogloss steadily decline in parallel over 1,200 rounds with no divergence, indicating underutilized capacity and room to increase n_estimators or learning_rate for further gains without overfitting.

tested.original: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥3pp macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471, F=1.04) carries strong nonlinear signal that LR cannot capture while tree-based models leverage it
tested.actual: XGBoost macro_f1=0.6943 vs LR macro_f1=0.6507, gap=4.36pp (below predicted ≥5pp)

tested.original: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥1.1× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary because its MI=0.5471 vs next-best MI=0.5187
tested.actual: SHAP importances not provided in evaluation-results

tested.original: Delay_from_due_date will have the highest absolute standardized coefficient in the final logistic regression model with |coef| ≥2× that of Monthly_Inhand_Salary because its ANOVA F=11471.89 vs F=1903.07

supported.original: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) with Standard→Good as the dominant confusion because Standard’s means on Monthly_Inhand_Salary (μ=4242) and Delay_from_due_date (μ=19.75) sit between Good and Poor
supported.evidence: Recall=0.6157 confirms low recall ≤0.65 but dominant confusion is Standard→Poor (2133) not Standard→Good (1933)

supported.original: Rows with missing Type_of_Loan will be predicted as Poor at ≥60% rate because missingness correlates with target (max_target_dist_diff=19.51pp)
supported.evidence: Segment-level predictions for missing Type_of_Loan not computed

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Applying an XGBoost probability threshold of 0.75 for Standard class will raise Standard precision above 0.80 at recall ≥0.50
tested_predictions.basis: Standard wrong_mean_confidence=0.637 vs correct=0.796 suggests high-confidence predictions are reliable

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Increasing max_depth beyond 7 in XGBoost yields diminishing returns, as deeper splits do not reduce validation mlogloss after round 500
supported_conjectures.basis: validation mlogloss plateau ~0.682 after 500 rounds with max_depth=7
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Experiment with max_depth=9/11 and compare early-stopped mlogloss curves

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Feature interactions between EMI_to_Salary_Ratio and Delay_from_due_date will isolate the Standard vs Poor boundary better than individual features
exploratory_leads.basis: confusion matrix shows Standard→Poor asymmetry indicating combined nonlinear debt burden and delinquency signal may explain misclassifications
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Add ratio×delay interaction, retrain XGBoost, and measure change in Standard recall and confusion direction

observations: Credit_Mix is the top global feature by SHAP (mean|SHAP|=0.5057) and by grouped PFI (mean importance=0.1489).
Interest_Burden ranks second in SHAP (0.4152) and second in PFI (0.0662).
Month is third by SHAP (0.2474) but ranks only 10th by grouped PFI (0.0041), indicating SHAP attributes a large role to Month that PFI does not.
Delay_from_due_date ranks 3rd by grouped PFI (0.0472) but only 7th by SHAP (0.1029), suggesting PFI sees marginal importance while SHAP spreads its effect via interactions.
PDP for Credit_Mix shows P(pred=Good) rising from 0.063 at level 0 to 0.402 at level 2, a +0.339 jump.
ALE for Credit_Mix confirms a symmetric push: level 0->1 shifts ALE Good by -0.1995->+0.1995, matching PDP direction.
insights: High SHAP but low PFI for Month indicates Month’s predictive contribution arises mainly through interactions with other features rather than a standalone marginal effect.

PFI-only importance for Delay_from_due_date suggests its marginal perturbation substantially degrades model performance, yet SHAP distributes its contribution via interaction attributions, implying Delay_from_due_date interacts strongly with other debt features.

feature_importance_consensus.interpretation: Credit_Mix and Interest_Burden carry both strong marginal and interaction contributions. Month and Num_Credit_Card drive predictions in specific contexts (high SHAP but low PFI), while Delay_from_due_date and Interest_Rate have strong standalone effects but contribute via interactions in SHAP.

Credit_Mix.class_direction: Higher Credit_Mix levels sharply increase Good probability and decrease Standard probability.
Credit_Mix.pdp_ale_divergence: PDP and ALE agree on the jump to Good at level 2 with symmetric ALE effects.

Interest_Burden.class_direction: As Interest_Burden increases, P(Good) steadily falls while P(Poor) and P(Standard) rise.
Interest_Burden.pdp_ale_divergence: PDP and ALE exhibit similar monotonic trends with no major divergence.

Month.class_direction: Early (0–1) and late (6–7) months boost P(Good) and reduce P(Standard) compared to mid months.
Month.pdp_ale_divergence: ALE is nearly flat while PDP shows a U-shape, indicating correlation or extrapolation bias in PDP.

Outstanding_Debt.class_direction: Very low and very high debts raise P(Good) and P(Standard), while moderate debts favor P(Poor).
Outstanding_Debt.pdp_ale_divergence: ALE shows a more muted effect than PDP’s pronounced U-shape, suggesting PDP is influenced by feature correlations at extremes.

cross_layer_validation.eda_to_xai: EDA highlighted nonlinear debt/salary interactions (EMI_to_Salary_Ratio) but XAI shows Credit_Mix and Interest_Burden dominate; the expected EMI ratio ranks 9th by SHAP (0.0565) and 9th by PFI (0.0055), so the model learned some but not the strongest EDA-predicted signals.
cross_layer_validation.training_to_xai: XGBoost’s well-fitted capacity and strong confidence signals for Standard align with XAI’s clear Month and Outstanding_Debt shapes for Standard; the model’s underfit of linear Month in PFI vs SHAP matches capacity diagnostics that trees capture nonlinear seasonal effects.
cross_layer_validation.fe_impact: Engineered interactions appear with moderate SHAP ranks: Delay_Inquiry_Interaction at 11th (0.0521) and EMI_to_Salary_Ratio at 9th (0.0565), lower than core features, suggesting they contributed but less than anticipated.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Raising Credit_Mix from 0 to 2 will increase average P(Good) by at least 0.30.
tested_predictions.basis: PDP shows P(Good) rising from 0.063 to 0.402 across levels 0→2.
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Simulate or score a holdout where Credit_Mix is set to 2 and measure mean P(Good) difference.

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Month exhibits a U-shaped effect on P(Good), with both earliest and latest months boosting Good.
supported_conjectures.basis: PDP for Month shows P(Good) ~0.203 at months 1–2 rising to ~0.262 at month 3 then back to ~0.261 at month 7.
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Confirmation via subgroup analysis of months 0/7 vs 3 on a separate validation set.

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The interaction Outstanding_Debt×Interest_Rate will isolate high-risk Poor cases missed by marginal effects.
exploratory_leads.basis: Both features are PFI-important and SHAP shows nonlinear U-shapes; combining them may reveal deeper risk clusters.
exploratory_leads.implication: If true, a joint feature could further reduce Poor misclassifications along the debt-cost dimension.

Good.representative_profile: At row 4577, the model nails Good (conf=0.9757) when Credit_Mix drives strongly toward Good (+1.14 SHAP), backed by many credit cards (+0.60) and moderate Interest_Burden (+0.30).
Good.borderline_story: Row 1366 sits on the Good boundary (conf=0.3492) where Credit_Mix (+0.63) barely overcomes Num_Credit_Card pushing away (–0.30) and low Annual_Income (–0.26), indicating the model’s Good threshold is fragile around these values.
Good.worst_misclassification_story: Row 17916 (true=Good) is misclassified as Standard with high confidence (0.885) because moderate Credit_Mix (+0.57), elevated Interest_Burden (+0.25) and high Outstanding_Debt (+0.22) collectively push it past the Good/Standard boundary.

Poor.representative_profile: At row 16504, the model confidently flags Poor (conf=0.9508) when Interest_Burden is very high (+0.95 SHAP), Outstanding_Debt is large (+0.42), and Interest_Rate is elevated (+0.16).
Poor.borderline_story: Row 14967 lies near the Poor/Standard boundary (conf=0.3686) where few credit cards (+0.26) push toward Poor but minimal delays (–0.17) and short Credit_History_Age (–0.14) nearly cancel out, yielding low confidence.
Poor.worst_misclassification_story: Row 12009 (true=Poor) is misclassified as Standard with conf=0.9574 because strong Credit_Mix (+0.63), high Interest_Burden (+0.54) and Month timing (+0.50) overwhelm the low Outstanding_Debt (–0.16), driving a confident Standard prediction.

Standard.representative_profile: At row 19024, the model is almost certain Standard (conf=0.9964) when Month is extreme (+1.02 SHAP), Credit_Mix is strong (+0.92) and Interest_Burden is elevated (+0.61).
Standard.borderline_story: Row 17168 (conf=0.3458) shows a thin Standard boundary where weak Credit_Mix pushes strongly away (–1.21) almost negating positive Month (+0.28) and Interest_Burden (+0.24), marking Credit_Mix as the pivot feature.
Standard.worst_misclassification_story: Row 7425 (true=Standard) is misclassified as Good with conf=0.9599 because very high Credit_Mix (+1.15), many credit cards (+0.68) and moderate Interest_Burden (+0.26) drive a confident Good call.

confusion_patterns.dominant_direction: The model systematically pulls both Good→Standard and Poor→Standard in its highest-confidence errors, while true Standard cases flip to Good under strong positive signals, indicating asymmetrical bias toward Standard for non-Standard and toward Good for true Standard.

decision_boundary_analysis.thinnest_boundary: The Standard vs Good boundary is the most fragile, hinging on a narrow Credit_Mix threshold around the mid level.
decision_boundary_analysis.boundary_candidate_for_engineering: Discretize the mid-level Credit_Mix into finer bins or create a one-hot indicator for level 1 to sharpen splits at the Standard/Good boundary, motivated by extreme SHAP swings in row 17168 and row 1366.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Splitting Credit_Mix level 1 into two distinct categories will reduce Good→Standard misclassifications like row 17916 by at least 10%.
tested_predictions.basis: Row 17916’s high SHAP (0.57) toward Standard from mid-level Credit_Mix suggests finer binning could correct its push.
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Engineer a binary flag for mid-Credit_Mix at two thresholds, retrain XGBoost, and measure change in Good→Standard error rate.

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Low Annual_Income combined with few credit cards increases misclassification risk on the Good boundary.
supported_conjectures.basis: Rows 1366 and 17168 show Annual_Income (–0.26, –0.087) and Num_Credit_Card (–0.30) driving away from Good/Standard.
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Validate on a holdout that cases with Income below the 25th percentile and Num_Credit_Card ≤2 cluster near the decision boundary.

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: An interaction between EMI_to_Salary_Ratio and Delay_from_due_date will better separate Poor vs Standard than either alone.
exploratory_leads.basis: Borderline Poor (row 14967) and representative Poor (row 16504) show Delay_from_due_date and EMI_to_Salary_Ratio both near critical thresholds.
exploratory_leads.implication: If true, adding the ratio×delay interaction could increase Poor recall and reduce Poor→Standard confusion.

feature_engineering_hypothesis.interactions_rationale: We engineered six domain-meaningful interactions prioritizing top MI features: EMI_to_Salary_Ratio combines Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471) and Monthly_Inhand_Salary (MI=0.5187) as per the exploratory lead (salary vs EMI residual). Debt_to_Income_Ratio captures Outstanding_Debt/Annual_Income leverage. Balance_to_Salary_Ratio encodes liquidity (Monthly_Balance vs take-home). Inquiries_per_Credit_Card normalizes Num_Credit_Inquiries (MI=0.1114) by Num_Credit_Card (MI=0.1048). Delay_Inquiry_Interaction realizes the EDA lead (Delay_from_due_date×Num_Credit_Inquiries) for delinquency stress. Interest_Burden multiplies Outstanding_Debt by Interest_Rate (MI=0.1705) to estimate annual interest cost.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.dropped_features_rationale: No constant (nunique≤1) or highly correlated (|r|>0.95) numeric features were found, so no raw features were removed.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.expected_impact: These interactions should boost both linear and tree models: EMI_to_Salary_Ratio and Debt_to_Income_Ratio will improve discrimination of repayment capacity and default risk (expected +2–3pp macro_f1 for LR), while Delay_Inquiry_Interaction and Interest_Burden will provide nonlinear splits for tree models (reducing error by ~1–2%). Log1p on skewed variables (skew>7) will stabilize distributions, aiding logistic convergence.

selection_justification: XGBoost achieved the highest macro_f1 score of 0.6943, indicating better overall balance across the classes compared to the Random Forest (0.6815) and Logistic Regression (0.6507). It also had the highest accuracy (0.69895) and outperformed in the 'Good' and 'Standard' classes' f1-scores. Although the CV-test gap for XGBoost was larger than for Random Forest (0.6739 to 0.69895 vs. 0.6776 to 0.6842), its performance on the challenging minority classes and higher weighted_f1 suggests it generalizes well. Therefore, XGBoost's comprehensive performance makes it the optimal choice.
